title: Understanding Medicare Telehealth
rawContent:
Understanding Medicare Telehealth Policy and Coverage Flexibilities in 2026

A decade ago, most Medicare beneficiaries could not see their doctor from home, at least not through a covered telehealth visit. The rules were strict, the eligible locations were limited, and the technology was largely ignored by the program. That changed dramatically starting in 2020, and the ripple effects are still reshaping how Medicare works today.

Telehealth refers to health care services delivered remotely through audio, video, or digital communication technologies. Before the COVID-19 Public Health Emergency, Medicare limited telehealth to rural beneficiaries who traveled to approved facilities. The PHE swept those restrictions aside, allowing millions to receive care from home.

As of 2026, those expanded flexibilities remain in place, extended through December 2027 by the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2026. Congress and CMS are actively working to determine which provisions become permanent, making this one of the most consequential policy debates in Medicare's recent history.

Key Takeaways

  • Medicare telehealth flexibilities have been extended through December 2027, giving beneficiaries continued access to remote care from their homes.

  • Both Traditional Medicare and Medicare Advantage cover telehealth, but Advantage plans often offer broader benefits and more flexible cost-sharing.

  • Congress is debating permanent telehealth expansion, and the outcome of that debate will significantly shape Medicare coverage in 2028 and beyond.

Current Scope of Medicare Telehealth Coverage: What's Covered Now?

Under Traditional Medicare (Part B), a wide range of services qualify for telehealth reimbursement in 2026. These include evaluation and management visits, mental health services, preventive care consultations, and certain chronic care management services. Eligible services are billed using standard CPT codes, and providers must meet specific documentation requirements to receive reimbursement.

The originating site where the patient is located, can now be the patient's home, a Federally Qualified Health Center, or a Rural Health Clinic, among other approved settings. Distant site providers who can bill for telehealth include physicians, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, clinical psychologists, clinical social workers, and certain licensed therapists.

Behavioral health telehealth received its own permanent expansion through the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2021. Mental health visits, including therapy and psychiatric evaluations, can now be conducted via telehealth without geographic restrictions. However, after an initial telehealth visit, most behavioral health providers must conduct an in-person visit within a set timeframe to maintain ongoing telehealth access.

Audio-only telehealth remains covered for beneficiaries who lack reliable internet access or video capability. This provision is especially significant for older adults on fixed incomes or those in rural areas with limited broadband. Audio-only visits are covered for certain services, though not all CPT codes qualify for audio-only billing.

Medicare Advantage Telehealth Benefits

Medicare Advantage plans operate under a different framework and often provide telehealth benefits that go beyond what Traditional Medicare covers. Many plans include expanded virtual visit options, lower copays for telehealth, and even same-day or on-demand video visits with network providers.

Some Medicare Advantage plans also include remote patient monitoring tools, devices that track blood pressure, glucose levels, or heart rate at home and transmit data to a care team. These benefits vary significantly from plan to plan, so reviewing your specific plan's Evidence of Coverage document is essential. You can learn more about what these plans include by exploring Medicare Advantage extra benefits in detail.

Navigating Telehealth Reimbursement: Payment Rates and Provider Considerations

Medicare pays for telehealth services through Part B, generally at rates comparable to in-person visits, though the specifics depend on where the patient is located and how the service is delivered. When a beneficiary receives telehealth from home, the payment is typically made at the non-facility rate, which is slightly higher than the facility rate paid when care is delivered in a hospital or clinic setting.

The 2026 Physician Fee Schedule Final Rule maintained parity for most telehealth reimbursements, preserving the payment levels established during the PHE period. This was a significant decision, as some providers feared rates would drop when emergency authority expired.

Service Setting

Payment Rate Type

Notes

Patient Home

Non-facility rate

Higher reimbursement; patient at home

Hospital / Clinic

Facility rate

Lower reimbursement; overhead shared

FQHC / RHC

Prospective payment

Separate cost-based methodology

Audio-Only Visit

Reduced non-facility rate

Only applies to eligible CPT codes

Providers must use the correct place-of-service codes and telehealth-specific modifiers when billing Medicare. Errors in coding are one of the most common reasons telehealth claims are denied or flagged for review. Staying current with CMS guidance on billing practices is essential for any provider offering virtual care.

Pro Tip

Before scheduling a telehealth appointment, call your provider's billing office and confirm they have your correct Medicare information on file and will use the appropriate telehealth billing codes. A simple administrative error, like a missing modifier, can cause a claim to be denied, leaving you with an unexpected bill. Taking two minutes to verify upfront can prevent weeks of back-and-forth later.

Beneficiary Utilization and Equity in Medicare Telehealth

Telehealth use among Traditional Medicare beneficiaries surged during the PHE and has remained substantially higher than pre-2020 levels. According to Medicare claims data through 2025, approximately 1 in 5 Part B beneficiaries used at least one telehealth service in a given year, a rate that would have been nearly unthinkable a decade ago. Mental health visits account for a disproportionately large share of ongoing telehealth use.

Dual-eligible beneficiaries, those enrolled in both Medicare and Medicaid, have shown particularly high telehealth utilization rates, especially for behavioral health services. Beneficiaries with End-Stage Renal Disease also benefit from telehealth provisions that allow certain dialysis-related consultations to be conducted remotely, reducing the burden of frequent in-person travel.

Geographic disparities persist, however. Urban beneficiaries still access telehealth at higher rates than rural counterparts, partly due to broadband availability gaps. The federal Broadband Equity, Access, and Deployment (BEAD) Program is actively working to close this gap, with funding allocated to expand high-speed internet infrastructure in underserved communities. As connectivity improves, telehealth access in rural areas is expected to increase meaningfully through 2027.

Race and Ethnicity in Telehealth Utilization

Telehealth utilization data broken down by race and ethnicity reveals notable disparities. White Medicare beneficiaries used video-based telehealth at higher rates than Black and Hispanic beneficiaries during the 2021–2024 period, while audio-only telehealth was more commonly used among Black beneficiaries, a pattern that reflects both technology access differences and provider communication preferences.

Contributing factors include lower rates of smartphone and broadband ownership, language barriers, and historically lower trust in health systems among some communities. CMS has responded with targeted outreach initiatives and by preserving audio-only coverage, which serves as a critical access point for beneficiaries without reliable video capabilities.

Ensuring equitable telehealth access remains a stated priority in CMS's annual rulemaking, and several pending legislative proposals include specific equity provisions tied to telehealth expansion. Closing these gaps is not just a fairness issue, it directly affects health outcomes for millions of Medicare enrollees.

Program Integrity and Medicare Spending in the Telehealth Era

The rapid expansion of telehealth created new opportunities for fraud, waste, and abuse. The Office of Inspector General (OIG) has documented multiple cases where unscrupulous providers billed Medicare for telehealth services that were never rendered, or used telehealth as a gateway to order unnecessary durable medical equipment and prescription drugs.

In one high-profile enforcement action, the OIG identified a scheme involving more than $1.1 billion in fraudulent Medicare telehealth claims, many submitted by providers who never had actual contact with beneficiaries. These cases underscore the importance of oversight even as Congress debates permanent expansion.

On the spending side, broader telehealth coverage has had a mixed impact on Medicare costs. Some analyses suggest telehealth reduces emergency room visits and hospitalizations, potentially offsetting costs. Others point to the risk of induced demand where easier access leads to more total visits without clear clinical benefit. CMS has responded by maintaining pre-authorization requirements for certain high-cost telehealth services and expanding data analytics to flag unusual billing patterns early. Understanding Medicare cost-sharing helps beneficiaries recognize their financial responsibilities under these services.

The Path to Permanent Medicare Telehealth: Legislative and Regulatory Outlook

The Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2026 extended most telehealth flexibilities through December 31, 2027. This two-year window gives Congress additional time to assess utilization data, cost impacts, and program integrity findings before making permanent decisions.

The most prominent legislative vehicle for permanent expansion is the CONNECT for Health Act, which has attracted bipartisan support in both chambers. The bill would permanently eliminate geographic restrictions on telehealth, make the patient's home a permanent originating site, and expand the list of eligible providers. Sponsored by a coalition of senators including supporters from both parties, it has been reintroduced in multiple congressional sessions and is widely seen as the most likely path to permanent reform.

Key policy debates center on cost neutrality, fraud prevention, and whether audio-only services should receive permanent parity. CMS and the Department of Health and Human Services are expected to issue new proposed rules in late 2026 or early 2027 to formalize the regulatory framework for whatever Congress ultimately authorizes. Beneficiaries can stay informed through Medicare policy changes as new rules are finalized.

Provider Perspectives: Adapting to Evolving Telehealth Policies

Federally Qualified Health Centers and Rural Health Clinics have been among the most active adopters of expanded telehealth. These safety-net providers serve low-income and geographically isolated populations, and telehealth has allowed them to extend their reach significantly. Both FQHCs and RHCs can now serve as distant sites, not just originating sites, a change that dramatically expands their capacity to deliver care.

Accountable Care Organizations gained specific telehealth flexibilities under the Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018, allowing ACO participants to use telehealth for more services without meeting traditional originating site requirements. These provisions were designed to support coordinated care models and reduce unnecessary hospitalizations.

For individual providers, the biggest challenges remain technology integration, staff training, and staying current with evolving billing rules. Providers who invest in reliable platforms and clear patient communication protocols tend to see better outcomes and fewer claim denials. Compliance with CMS documentation standards, including proper consent capture and visit documentation, is non-negotiable for sustainable telehealth practice.

Future Trends and Impact on Patient Care

Several emerging technologies are poised to further reshape Medicare telehealth. Remote patient monitoring devices are becoming more sophisticated, enabling continuous tracking of chronic conditions like heart failure and diabetes between visits. Artificial intelligence tools are being integrated into telehealth platforms to assist with triage, documentation, and follow-up scheduling.

Early outcome data comparing telehealth to in-person care shows comparable results for many primary care and behavioral health services. Patients managing chronic conditions through regular telehealth check-ins report high satisfaction rates, and some studies suggest reduced hospitalization rates among engaged telehealth users.

If Congress passes permanent legislation before the 2027 deadline, the structural changes to Medicare delivery could be profound. Vulnerable populations, including homebound seniors, those with mobility limitations, and rural beneficiaries, stand to benefit most. The long-term trajectory points toward a hybrid care model where telehealth and in-person visits complement each other, guided by Medicare telehealth coverage rules that continue to evolve.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

  1. Assuming all telehealth visits are covered the same way. Not every service qualifies for telehealth reimbursement, and audio-only visits have a narrower list of eligible CPT codes than video visits. Always verify coverage before your appointment rather than assuming the visit will be billed correctly.

  2. Ignoring the in-person visit requirement for behavioral health. After starting behavioral health telehealth care, many beneficiaries don't realize an in-person visit with the provider is eventually required to continue telehealth services. Skipping this requirement can interrupt your care and create billing complications.

  3. Not reviewing your Medicare Advantage plan's specific telehealth rules. Medicare Advantage plans each set their own telehealth policies, network requirements, and cost-sharing structures. Using an out-of-network provider for a telehealth visit could result in full out-of-pocket costs, even if the service itself is covered.

  4. Treating the 2027 extension as a guarantee of permanent coverage. The current flexibilities expire December 31, 2027. Congress has not yet passed permanent legislation. Beneficiaries who rely heavily on telehealth should monitor legislative developments and plan accordingly.

  5. Failing to confirm provider credentials for telehealth billing. Not all provider types can bill Medicare for every type of telehealth service. A provider who is ineligible to bill for a particular telehealth service may inadvertently pass the cost to you. Confirming your provider's Medicare enrollment status protects you from surprise charges.

  6. Overlooking broadband-related telehealth access programs. Many beneficiaries don't know that assistance programs, including federal broadband subsidies, may help cover internet access costs. Better connectivity directly expands your telehealth options, so ignoring these programs means leaving available resources unused.

Frequently Asked Questions About Medicare Telehealth in 2026

What is the current status of Medicare telehealth coverage in 2026?

Most expanded telehealth flexibilities remain in place through December 31, 2027, extended by the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2026. Beneficiaries can continue to receive covered telehealth services from their homes without geographic restrictions.

Are audio-only telehealth visits still covered by Medicare?

Yes. Audio-only telehealth visits remain covered for a defined set of services, particularly for beneficiaries who lack video capability or reliable internet. Not every service type qualifies for audio-only billing, so confirm eligibility with your provider.

How do Medicare Advantage telehealth benefits differ from Traditional Medicare?

Medicare Advantage plans can offer broader telehealth benefits, including on-demand video visits, remote monitoring, and lower cost-sharing than Traditional Medicare provides. Benefits vary by plan, so reviewing your plan's Evidence of Coverage document is essential.

What are the requirements for providers to bill Medicare for telehealth services?

Providers must be enrolled in Medicare, use approved CPT codes with correct place-of-service designations, and document the visit according to CMS standards. The provider type must also be on Medicare's approved distant site provider list.

Will telehealth flexibilities become permanent after 2027?

That decision rests with Congress. Bipartisan legislation like the CONNECT for Health Act has broad support, but no permanent law has been passed as of 2026. The 2027 deadline creates urgency for a final legislative resolution.

How does Medicare ensure program integrity with expanded telehealth?

CMS uses data analytics to flag unusual billing patterns, and the OIG conducts targeted audits of high-volume telehealth billers. Providers submitting fraudulent claims face civil and criminal penalties, including exclusion from Medicare.

Can I receive telehealth services from my home under Medicare?

Yes. The patient's home is currently an approved originating site under the extended telehealth flexibilities. This provision is one of the most impactful changes from the PHE era and remains in place through 2027.

What is the difference between an originating site and a distant site provider?

The originating site is where the patient is located during the telehealth visit, such as your home. The distant site is where the treating provider is located. Both must meet Medicare's eligibility requirements for a visit to be covered.

Conclusion: Shaping the Future of Medicare Telehealth

Medicare telehealth has traveled a remarkable distance, from a narrowly restricted benefit available only in rural clinics to a flexible, widely used service that millions of beneficiaries access from home. The extensions through 2027 preserve that access while giving policymakers time to build a permanent framework.

Balancing access, equity, program integrity, and cost remains the central challenge. The decisions made before December 2027 will define how Medicare delivers care for decades. Staying informed is the best thing you can do right now. If you have questions about your specific coverage, explore our Medicare benefits resources or connect with a licensed agent who can walk you through your options in plain language.
excerpt: Learn about Medicare telehealth policy and coverage, including current flexibilities extended through 2027. Discover what's covered and what's next for remote healthcare.
author: David Haass
keyTakeaways: Medicare telehealth flexibilities are extended through December 2027, ensuring continued access to remote care from home.
Both Traditional Medicare and Medicare Advantage cover telehealth, with Advantage plans often providing broader benefits.
Congress is actively debating permanent telehealth expansion, which will significantly impact Medicare coverage from 2028.
A wide range of services, including mental health and preventive care, qualify for telehealth reimbursement under Traditional Medicare.
Patients can receive telehealth from home, and various providers are eligible to bill for distant site services.
